### Post by jlacroix on 2013-06-16
Hello everyone, I'm setting up a server in my lab so I can try to get better at networking in Linux. I am new to iptables. I ran through a few tutorials and I was able to get routing working between my two NIC's. However, the port forwarding portion doesn't seem to work. Port 65001 is the one that the server should be listening on. When I come in to my home network via a remote connection, it will time out.

```
# Generated by iptables-save v1.4.14 on Sun Jun 16 15:42:56 2013
*mangle
:PREROUTING ACCEPT [5311:1687958]
:INPUT ACCEPT [1731:194586]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [3580:1493372]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [1129:131055]
:POSTROUTING ACCEPT [4732:1627866]
COMMIT
# Completed on Sun Jun 16 15:42:56 2013
# Generated by iptables-save v1.4.14 on Sun Jun 16 15:42:56 2013
*nat
:PREROUTING ACCEPT [159:10814]
:INPUT ACCEPT [73:5654]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [114:8214]
:POSTROUTING ACCEPT [0:0]
-A PREROUTING -i lo -p tcp -m tcp --dport 65001 -j DNAT --to-destination 172.16.254.1:65001
-A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE
COMMIT
# Completed on Sun Jun 16 15:42:56 2013
# Generated by iptables-save v1.4.14 on Sun Jun 16 15:42:56 2013
*filter
:INPUT ACCEPT [976:111278]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [1120:807222]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [662:79869]
-A FORWARD -d 172.16.254.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 65001 -j ACCEPT
-A FORWARD -i eth1 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT
# Completed on Sun Jun 16 15:42:56 2013

```

Is there something that I'm missing?

---

### Post by darkod on 2013-06-16
You need a PREROUTING DNAT rule for any port you want routed/forwarded, in the *nat section. Also, you don't need the INPUT, FORWARD and OUTPUT policies in the *nat section, only in the *filter section. The *nat section usually only works with PREROUTING and POSTROUTING rules.

You only have a prerouting rule for the lo interface. If eth0 is the internet interface, and eth1 is the internal interface, in the *nat section you need something like:
-A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 65001 -j DNAT --to-destination 172.16.254.1

Also, in the *filter section you don't need any special FORWARD rules since your FORWARD policy is ACCEPT. If you set it to DROP, you need specific FORWARD rules to accept traffic you want to accept to be forwarded. With the policy being ACCEPT, it will accept any forward traffic anyway. Of course, any forward traffic also needs a DNAT rule in *nat.

---

### Post by jlacroix on 2013-06-16

### Post by darkod on 2013-06-16
1. Is forwarding enabled in /etc/sysctl.conf? You have to enable the net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 line (remove the # at front). Save and close the file, restart the networking or the whole server. This is needed for packets to be forwarded between interfaces.

2. Is the ssh server listening on port 65001?

---

### Post by jlacroix on 2013-06-16
> **darkod said:**
> 1. Is forwarding enabled in /etc/sysctl.conf? You have to enable the net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 line (remove the # at front). Save and close the file, restart the networking or the whole server. This is needed for packets to be forwarded between interfaces.

2. Is the ssh server listening on port 65001?
The answer to both questions is yes.

### Post by sanderj on 2013-06-17
I would take these steps:

1) disable the firewall, and check that the port forwarding is working
2) enable the firewall with everything on PERMIT, and check that the port forwarding is working
3) start "closing" the firewall

---

### Post by darkod on 2013-06-17
I just noticed later on that you actually have the firewall disabled because in the *filter section you have all INPUT, FORWARD and OUTPUT with ACCEPT policy. So, the firewall part is disabled.
The port forwarding should work.

Are you sure the packets are entering correctly in the eth0 interface? You mentioned you have a cable modem connected to it. Usually a cable modem can require that you set up a PPPoE or Static IP connection on the gateway external interface (eth0). I assume this is set up correctly. Does the gateway/firewall server have internet access? Have you tested it?

Also, is the gateway machine specified as gateway to the clients on the LAN?

---

Last weekend I put together an Intel Atom server to use as my router instead. Mostly for learning purposes but I'm noticing a HUGE speed boost when the computers in my house connect to the Internet.

So yes, eth0 is the cable modem, and eth1 is connected to a 24-port switch. The gateway does have Internet access, as do all of the computers in my house. Yes, the computer is specified as the gateway to all the machines.

But it bothers me that you said that my firewall is effectively disabled. I found the following article and I followed step 8 to set it up:
[http://linuxpoison.blogspot.tw/2009/02/how-to-configure-linux-as-internet.html?m=1](http://linuxpoison.blogspot.tw/2009/02/how-to-configure-linux-as-internet.html?m=1)

Essentially what I want to achieve is to block everything except for standard Internet traffic and also allow port 65001 and maybe also 65010. (I thought that was what that tutorial was walking me through doing). I may need to open ports for my PS3 or Netflix, but then again, with my wireless router I never had to allow any ports for those to work and UPnP was disabled.

### Post by darkod on 2013-06-17
Look at your *filter policy settings. As soon as you have INPUT ACCEPT, anyone is accepted into your gateway (because the INPUT is only for local traffic entering the gateway/server itself, not the traffic forwarded (passing) through it.

Locking the server and leaving http and https (80 and 443) accepted, and also 65001 for ssh, would be like:
```
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]

-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
-A FORWARD -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

COMMIT
```

That would be the basic that blocks any traffic in INPUT and FORWARD by policy, letting in only what you specify with rules. The first basic rules are to let in back all established and related traffic, so your gateway and clients can communicate.

When you say you want 80 and 443 let in, do you run services on a server on your LAN? Otherwise, your clients will be able to browse just fine, you don't need to open 80 and 443 for incoming traffic. Your clients make outgoing traffic, and that will be let in by the established,related rule.

If you are running web services you need to allow the ports and also forward them to the correct web server private IP on your LAN (in the *nat section):
-A FORWARD -i eth0 -p tcp -m multiport --dports 80,443,65001 -j ACCEPT

That should let in ports 80,443 and 65001. I hope I used the multiport syntax correctly.

If you are only interested in ports 80 and 443 so that your home machines can browse, you will have to allow the forward traffic in the other direction (inside to outside), something like:
-A FORWARD -i eth1 -s 172.16.254.0/24 -p tcp -m multiport --dports 80,443 -j ACCEPT

One option, as a temporary solution, is to have the forward policy to ACCEPT, check that everything is working fine, and then change it to DROP and start adding rules until everything works as you want to. In your rules design, just follow the traffic flow.

If you set forward and output to ACCEPT, you don't need to do much except make the DNAT rule for the ssh port. And DNAT rules for http and https if you are running a webserver at home. All the other traffic will be accepted by the forward ACCEPT policy anyway.

But as soon as you set forward to DROP, you have to be ready and prepare rules for all traffic your home machines might need, that was previously accepted by the ACCEPT policy. But that shouldn't be too hard. It might be trial and error a bit, until you get it right. :)

---

### Post by jlacroix on 2013-06-18
> **darkod said:**
> Look at your *filter policy settings. As soon as you have INPUT ACCEPT, anyone is accepted into your gateway (because the INPUT is only for local traffic entering the gateway/server itself, not the traffic forwarded (passing) through it.

Locking the server and leaving http and https (80 and 443) accepted, and also 65001 for ssh, would be like:
```
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]

-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
-A FORWARD -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

COMMIT
```

That would be the basic that blocks any traffic in INPUT and FORWARD by policy, letting in only what you specify with rules. The first basic rules are to let in back all established and related traffic, so your gateway and clients can communicate.

When you say you want 80 and 443 let in, do you run services on a server on your LAN? Otherwise, your clients will be able to browse just fine, you don't need to open 80 and 443 for incoming traffic. Your clients make outgoing traffic, and that will be let in by the established,related rule.

If you are running web services you need to allow the ports and also forward them to the correct web server private IP on your LAN (in the *nat section):
-A FORWARD -i eth0 -p tcp -m multiport --dports 80,443,65001 -j ACCEPT

That should let in ports 80,443 and 65001. I hope I used the multiport syntax correctly.

If you are only interested in ports 80 and 443 so that your home machines can browse, you will have to allow the forward traffic in the other direction (inside to outside), something like:
-A FORWARD -i eth1 -s 172.16.254.0/24 -p tcp -m multiport --dports 80,443 -j ACCEPT

One option, as a temporary solution, is to have the forward policy to ACCEPT, check that everything is working fine, and then change it to DROP and start adding rules until everything works as you want to. In your rules design, just follow the traffic flow.

If you set forward and output to ACCEPT, you don't need to do much except make the DNAT rule for the ssh port. And DNAT rules for http and https if you are running a webserver at home. All the other traffic will be accepted by the forward ACCEPT policy anyway.

But as soon as you set forward to DROP, you have to be ready and prepare rules for all traffic your home machines might need, that was previously accepted by the ACCEPT policy. But that shouldn't be too hard. It might be trial and error a bit, until you get it right. :)
I just wanted to say, thank you VERY MUCH for your help. By using your guidance as a base, I was able to create a script that sets up all my rules, with security in mind. I checked my new policy against Shields Up and all ports don't respond to outside pings, but my internal SSH and web traffic seems to work fine. I am sure there will be more ports that I will have to open as I go, but after four hours of intense trial and error testing all my network stuff, the script below is what I ended up with (hopefully it will help someone else):

```
#!/bin/bash
# init

## Flush current configuration:
iptables -F
iptables -t nat -F
iptables -t mangle -F

## Delete current chains:
iptables -X
iptables -t nat -X
iptables -t mangle -X

## Set policy
iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A FORWARD -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

## Allow routing between eth0 and eth1
iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE
iptables -A FORWARD -i eth1 -j ACCEPT

## Allow internal SSH:
iptables -A INPUT -s 172.16.254.0/24 -m state --state NEW -p tcp -m multiport --dports 65001,65010 -j ACCEPT

## Allow external SSH:
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -m multiport --dports 65001,65010 -j ACCEPT

## Allow port forwarding for SSH:
iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 65001 -j DNAT --to-destination 172.16.254.1
iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 65010 -j DNAT --to-destination 172.16.254.10

## Required for internal host name resolution to function:
iptables -I INPUT -s 172.16.254.0/24 -p udp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
iptables -I INPUT -s 172.16.254.0/24 -p tcp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT

## Required for Samba to function:
iptables -A INPUT -s 172.16.254.0/24 -m state --state NEW -p tcp -m multiport --dports 137,138,139,445 -j ACCEPT

iptables-save > /etc/iptables.rules

```

Now I'm on my way to begin learning iptables! :)
